questions.question: Explain the fundamental differences between SISD, SIMD, and MIMD architectures under Flynn's Taxonomy. Provide one practical example for each category.
keyPoints: Define SISD: single instruction on single data stream - sequential computers
Define SIMD: single instruction on multiple data streams - vector processors, GPUs
Define MIMD: multiple instructions on multiple data streams - multi-core systems
Examples: SISD = Intel Core i7 single thread, SIMD = NVIDIA GPU, MIMD = distributed cluster

questions.question: Differentiate between Uniform Memory Access (UMA) and Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA) architectures. Discuss which architecture is more suitable for parallel workloads and why.
keyPoints: UMA: all processors have equal access time to memory via shared interconnect
NUMA: access time depends on distance - local memory faster than remote
NUMA advantages: scales better, reduced memory bus contention
NUMA disadvantages: programming complexity, need to manage data locality

questions.question: What is the key distinction between data parallelism and task parallelism? Provide examples of algorithms or problems where each paradigm would be most appropriate.
keyPoints: Data parallelism: same operation on different data elements concurrently
Task parallelism: different operations/tasks executed simultaneously
Data parallel examples: matrix operations, image processing, Monte Carlo
Task parallel examples: pipeline processing, tree search, client-server

questions.question: State and explain Amdahl's Law. If a program has 95% parallelizable code, what is the maximum speedup achievable with 64 processors? Show your calculation.
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law: S = 1/(1-P) where P is parallel fraction
Theoretical maximum speedup = 1/(1-0.95) = 1/0.05 = 20x
Note: This is theoretical; practical speedup will be lower due to overhead
Even with infinite processors, speedup limited by serial portion

questions.question: Compare snooping-based and directory-based cache coherence protocols. Explain why directory-based protocols are more scalable for large-scale systems.
keyPoints: Snooping: caches monitor bus transactions, broadcast updates/invalidations
Directory: centralized directory tracks sharing status, point-to-point messages
Snooping limitations: bus bandwidth bottleneck, doesn't scale beyond 16-32 processors
Directory advantages: scalable, no broadcast traffic, handles hundreds of processors

questions.question: Define the SPMD execution model and explain why it is widely used in parallel programming despite the availability of SIMD hardware. How does SPMD differ from MPMD?
keyPoints: SPMD: Single Program Multiple Data - same program runs on all processors with different data
Allows conditional execution and dynamic branching unlike SIMD
Each process has unique ID to take different paths
MPMD: Multiple Programs Multiple Data - different programs may run on different processors
SPMD is common in MPI and thread-based programming

questions.question: A parallel application exhibits the following characteristics: 80% of the workload can be parallelized, and communication overhead adds 5% to the parallel portion's execution time. (a) Calculate the theoretical maximum speedup with 16 processors using Amdahl's Law. (b) If the actual measured speedup is 7x, calculate the parallel efficiency. (c) Explain three possible reasons for the discrepancy between theoretical and actual speedup.
keyPoints: (a) Effective P = 0.80 × 1.05 = 0.84 (accounting for overhead); S = 1/(1-0.84) = 1/0.16 = 6.25x
(b) Efficiency = Speedup/Processors = 7/16 = 43.75%

(c) Reasons: (i) Load imbalance - not all work equally distributed, (ii) Communication overhead - more significant at scale, (iii) Synchronization delays - barriers create idle time, (iv) Memory bandwidth limitations, (v) Cache effects - false sharing, coherency traffic

questions.question: Design a hybrid parallel system architecture for a weather simulation application that needs to process 1000 × 1000 grid points with atmospheric physics calculations. Justify your choice of hardware architecture, memory organization, and programming model. Consider factors like data locality, load balancing, and communication patterns.
keyPoints: Hardware: Hybrid CPU-GPU or distributed cluster with multi-core nodes
Memory: NUMA-aware local memory per node, shared memory within node
Programming: MPI between nodes (distributed memory), OpenMP/CUDA within nodes (shared memory)
Grid decomposition: Divide grid into chunks assigned to processors
Communication: Halo exchange at boundaries between time steps
Load balancing: Dynamic redistribution if atmospheric conditions vary spatially
Data locality: Keep computation near local memory to minimize remote access

questions.question: Given a hypercube interconnection network with 32 nodes: (a) What is the diameter of this network? (b) How many links does each node have? (c) Calculate the bisection bandwidth if each link provides 10 Gbps? (d) Explain why diameter and bisection bandwidth are important metrics for parallel system performance.
keyPoints: (a) Diameter = log₂(32) = 5 for hypercube with 2^5 nodes
(b) Each node degree = 5 (connects to 5 other nodes)
(c) Bisection: N/2 links must be cut = 16 × 5/2 = 40 links; Bandwidth = 40 × 10 Gbps = 400 Gbps
(d) Diameter affects worst-case latency for communication
Bisection bandwidth limits all-to-all communication patterns
Lower diameter and higher bisection bandwidth improve performance
These metrics determine scalability of the interconnection network
